title: Clinical outcome of pingyangmycin-polidocanol foam versus pingyangmycin alone in pediatric cystic lymphatic malformation with intracapsular hemorrhage: A retrospective cohort study.
abstract: Cystic lymphatic malformations (CLMs) with intracapsular hemorrhage present a management challenge. This study aimed to describe and compare the clinical outcomes associated with pingyangmycin-polidocanol foam (PPF) versus pingyangmycin (PYM) sclerotherapy in this pediatric population. A retrospective cohort study was conducted on 74 pediatric patients with CLMs and intracapsular hemorrhage treated between October 2020 and May 2023. Patients were treated with either PPF (n = 37) or PYM (n = 37). The primary outcomes were lesion volume reduction grading, number of treatment sessions, pain relief, and family satisfaction. Secondary outcomes included adverse effects and recurrence. Factors associated with treatment response were explored. Baseline characteristics were comparable between groups. Children in the PPF group exhibited a greater median reduction in lesion volume after the first session (23.0 cm3 vs 12.2 cm3, P = .028) and received fewer median treatment sessions (2 vs 3, P = .023) compared with those in the PYM group. A higher proportion in the PPF group achieved a good response (Achauer grade III/IV) after the final treatment (83.8% vs 73.0%, P = .020) and reported complete pain relief (73.6% vs 41.7%, P = .036). Family satisfaction scores were significantly higher in the PPF group (P = .020). There were no significant differences in overall adverse event rates (18.9% vs 27.0%, P = .407) or recurrence rates (21.6% vs 27.0%, P = .787). Multivariable analysis confirmed that PPF treatment and macrocystic/mixed lesion type were independent factors associated with a good response. An increased number of treatment sessions was associated with more adverse effects but lower recurrence. In this retrospective analysis, PPF sclerotherapy was associated with a greater initial volume reduction, fewer treatment sessions, and higher family satisfaction compared with PYM alone in pediatric CLMs with intracapsular hemorrhage. Macrocystic and mixed lesions showed better response to PPF. Prospective studies are warranted to validate these findings.

full_text_sections.1. INTRODUCTION: 1. Introduction Cystic lymphatic malformation (CLM) is a congenital vascular disorder characterized by excessive lymphatic tissue growth during development. [ 1 ] Approximately half of CLM cases are diagnosed at birth, with 80% to 90% identified within the first 2 years of life. [ 1 ] The incidence rate of CLMs is approximately 1 in 2000 to 4000 live births, with an equal distribution between male and female patients. [ 2 ] This benign disease can persist throughout life, progressing slowly and proportionally with growth. The clinical presentation of CLMs varies depending on the type, location, extent, and depth of the lesions, which in turn influence the efficacy of sclerotherapy. These malformations can develop in various parts of the body, particularly in areas rich in lymphatic vessels, especially the head and neck, but are not found in the central nervous system. [ 3 – 5 ] Based on the size of the lymphatic cystic cavity, CLMs are classified into 3 categories: macrocystic (cysts ≥ 2 cm 3 or diameter > 1 cm), microcystic, and mixed. [ 5 – 7 ] CLMs are typically soft, painless masses but can undergo sudden enlargement due to intracapsular hemorrhage or infection, leading to pain, skin discoloration, and compression of adjacent structures. [ 5 , 8 ] Hemorrhage in critical locations can cause airway obstruction, visual impairment, or functional deficits. [ 4 , 5 , 9 ] Sclerotherapy has become a first-line treatment for CLMs, offering advantages over surgery such as lower morbidity and better cosmetic outcomes. [ 10 , 11 ] Various sclerosants are used, including pingyangmycin (PYM, bleomycin A5) and polidocanol. [ 5 , 12 ] PYM inhibits DNA synthesis and promotes fibrosis of the endothelial lining. [ 13 ] Polidocanol is a detergent sclerosant that damages endothelial cell membranes. [ 14 ] The combination of these agents in a foam formulation (pingyangmycin–polidocanol foam [PPF]) may theoretically enhance efficacy by increasing drug-endothelial contact time and distribution. [ 15 ] While prior studies have examined individual or combined agents for CLMs, [ 16 , 17 ] data specifically focusing on the subset with active intracapsular hemorrhage are limited. Additionally, this study aimed to describe and compare the clinical outcomes associated with PPF versus PYM sclerotherapy in pediatric CLMs with intracapsular hemorrhage and to explore factors associated with treatment response.
full_text_sections.2. METHODS: 2. Methods 2.1. Study population and design This was a single-center retrospective cohort study. Children diagnosed with CLMs complicated by intracapsular hemorrhage who underwent sclerotherapy at our hospital between October 2020 and May 2023 were included. Diagnosis required pre-procedural ultrasound or magnetic resonance imaging, suggesting intracystic hemorrhage (e.g., fluid–fluid level) and confirmationvia intraoperative aspiration of dark red, non-coagulable fluid. [ 18 ] This study used the clinically practical diameter-based classification (>1 cm) for macrocystic lesions, consistent with common interventional radiology practice. [ 5 , 6 , 11 ] Lesion depth was categorized as superficial (subcutaneous layer) or deep (deep fascia or muscle layer). 2.2. Inclusion and exclusion criteria The inclusion criteria for this study were as follows: diagnosis of CLMs with intracapsular hemorrhage as defined above; age ≤18 years; treatment with either PPF or PYM monotherapy at our center; and availability of complete pre- and post-treatment imaging and clinical records. Exclusion criteria were as follows: incomplete sclerotherapy due to allergy or other complications; diagnosis of other major concurrent diseases or high-flow vascular malformations; and loss to follow-up before the 6-month assessment. Based on the inclusion criteria, 96 patients with CLMs and intracapsular hemorrhage were initially screened. Of these, 22 were excluded due to incomplete medical records (n = 13), loss to follow-up before the 6-month assessment (n = 7), or incomplete sclerotherapy due to allergy (n = 2), leaving a final cohort of 74 patients for analysis, who were divided into 2 groups: patients received PPF sclerosing agent (PPF group) and patients received PYM single sclerosing agent (PYM group). 2.3. Sclerotherapy 2.3.1. Treatment allocation and sclerotherapy procedure The choice of sclerosing agent (PPF or PYM) was based on the treating physician’s clinical judgment, considering factors such as lesion morphology, anticipated treatment complexity, and institutional protocol evolution. The PYM monotherapy was more common in the earlier phase of the study period, while the use of PPF increased in the latter phase as experience with the foam technique grew. All procedures were performed under general anesthesia with standard preoperative assessment. For the PPF group, foam was prepared using the Tessari method [ 19 ] : 8 mg PYM, 2 mL of 1% polidocanol, and 5 mg dexamethasone were mixed with 8 mL air (liquid: gas ratio 1:4) by agitation. For the PYM group, 8 mg PYM and 5 mg dexamethasone were dissolved in 4 mL of normal saline. Dosage was based on lesion size. The maximum single dose was 4 mg for PYM and 2 mg/kg for polidocanol foam. [ 13 , 20 ] Post-procedure compression was applied. Under digital subtraction angiography guidance, multi-point and multi-angle percutaneous puncture was performed at the lesion site using a 4.5 scalp needle, with successful identification marked by dark red non-coagulated blood flow. The sac fluid was drained as thoroughly as possible, followed by percutaneous injection of the composite foam hardener into the capsule under digital subtraction angiography fluoroscopic guidance. Post-procedure imaging confirmed the deposition of the hardener within the capsule. After needle removal, the lesion was compressed for 5 minutes to control bleeding and prevent agent leakage, followed by local pressure bandaging for 1 to 2 days. 2.4. Observation indicators Lesion volume reduction was estimated from pre- and post-treatment ultrasound or magnetic resonance imaging. For well-defined cysts, the ellipsoid formula (length × width × height × π/6) was used. For complex lesions, volumetric analysis was performed via manual tracing on sequential imaging slices. Efficacy was graded using Achauer criteria: grade I (0%–25% reduction), II (26%–50%), III (51%–75%), and IV (76%–100%). [ 21 ] A “good response” was defined as grade III or IV after the final treatment session. A simple, non-validated 4-point scale was used in the clinic to capture global family satisfaction across domains of appearance, daily activity, and social interaction (3 = improved in all, 2 = improved in 2, 1 = improved in one, 0 = no improvement). A score of 2 or 3 was considered “satisfied.” Pain relief was defined as the absence of patient-reported or parent-observed pain at the lesion site at the last follow-up. Adverse reactions (such as fever, swelling, skin pigmentation at the injection site, and gastrointestinal reactions) were documented within 1 week posttreatment. A recurrence event was defined as a >50% increase in lesion volume from the posttreatment nadir on imaging during follow-up, indicating clinically significant regrowth. [ 22 ] 2.5. Follow-up Patients were followed clinically and with ultrasound at 4 to 6 weeks posttreatment and then every 3 to 6 months. The minimum follow-up period for this analysis was 6 months after the final sclerotherapy session. Throughout the follow-up period, detailed records were maintained on changes in lesion volume, pain relief, recurrence events, adverse reactions, and family satisfaction. 2.6. Ethics The study was approved by the Ethical Committee of Capital Institute of Pediatrics (SHERLL2022001). For this retrospective analysis of anonymized data, the requirement for informed consent was waived by the ethics committee. However, all therapeutic procedures were performed after obtaining written informed consent from parents or guardians. 2.7. Statistical analysis Data were analyzed using IBM SPSS Statistics (version 25.0; IBM Corp., Armonk). Continuous data with normal distribution are presented as mean ± standard deviation and compared using Student’s t test; non-normally distributed data are presented as median (interquartile range) and compared using the Mann–Whitney U test. Categorical data are presented as frequencies (percentages) and compared using the chi-square test or Fisher exact test.
full_text_sections.3. RESULTS: 3. Results 3.1. Baseline demographic and clinical characteristics Seventy-four patients met the inclusion criteria, with 37 in each group. Baseline characteristics were well balanced between the PPF and PYM groups, with no significant differences in age, gender, lesion type, location, pretreatment volume, depth, or presenting symptoms of pain and skin discoloration (all P > .05), as detailed in Table 1 . Table 1 Baseline demographic and clinical characteristics of the study cohort. Characteristic PPF (n = 37) PYM (n = 37) P Age of initial treatment (mo) Median (IQR) 37.0 (18.0–62.0) 35.0 (22.0–61.0) .782 Gender, n (%) .812 Male 20 (54.1) 19 (51.4) Female 17 (45.9) 18 (48.6) Type, n (%) .864 Macrocystic 17 (45.9) 19 (51.4) Microcystic 8 (21.6) 8 (21.6) Mixed 12 (32.4) 10 (27.0) Location affected, n (%) .924 Head and neck 18 (48.6) 16 (43.2) Trunk 11 (29.7) 14 (37.8) Limb 9 (24.3) 9 (24.3) Axilla 2 (5.4) 2 (5.4) Pretreatment volume (cm 3 ) Median (IQR) 31.9 (12.8–68.0) 23.1 (6.1–71.3) .765 Maximum diameter (cm) Median (IQR) 4.1 (2.4–5.1) 4.8 (2.8–6.2) .613 Depth of invasion, n (%) .642 Superficial 20 (54.1) 18 (48.6) Deep 17 (45.9) 19 (51.4) Presenting with pain, n (%) 19 (51.4) 24 (64.9) .239 Presenting with skin discoloration, n (%) 22 (59.5) 19 (51.4) .483 P < .05 was considered statistically significant. IQR = interquartile range, PPF = pingyangmycin–polidocanol foam, PYM = pingyangmycin. 3.2. Comparative treatment outcomes Outcomes are summarized in Table 2 . The PPF group showed a significantly greater median volume reduction after the first treatment session. The median number of sessions required to achieve the treatment endpoint was lower in the PPF group. After the final treatment, a higher proportion of patients in the PPF group achieved a good response (grade III/IV). Complete pain relief and family satisfaction rates were also significantly higher in the PPF group. Among the patients in the PPF group, 7 experienced immediate treatment-related adverse events, including 3 cases with fever, 3 cases with swelling, and 1 case with pigmentation at the injection site. However, the PYM group had 4 patients with fever, 3 with significant swelling, 2 with pigmentation at the injection site, and 1 with a gastrointestinal reaction ( P = .407). There were 8 cases with recurrence in the PPF group and 10 in the PYM group, and both groups underwent sclerotherapy again ( P = .787). The incidence of adverse events and recurrence rates were not statistically different between the 2 groups. Table 2 Comparison of treatment outcomes between PPF and PYM groups. Features PPF (n = 37) PYM (n = 37) P Volume reduction after first session Median reduction (cm 3 ), (IQR) 23.0 (8.9–62.1) 12.2 (4.7–24.6) .028 Number of injections Median (IQR) 2 (1–4) 3 (1–7) .023 Final treatment response (Achauer grade), n (%) Grade I/II (poor) 6 (16.2) 10 (27.0) .020 Grade III/IV (good) 31 (83.8) 27 (73.0) Pain relief (in those presenting with pain), n/N (%) 14/19 (73.6) 10/24 (41.7) .036 Family satisfaction (score 2 or 3), n (%) 35 (94.6) 28 (75.7) .020 Adverse events, n (%) 7 (18.9) 10 (27.0) .407 Recurrence, n (%) 8 (21.6) 10 (27.0) .787 P < .05 was considered statistically significant. IQR = interquartile range, PPF = pingyangmycin–polidocanol foam, PYM = pingyangmycin. 3.3. Factors associated with good treatment response: multivariable analysis Multivariable logistic regression analysis (Table 3 ) identified 2 independent factors significantly associated with achieving a final good response (grade III/IV). Treatment with PPF versus PYM: adjusted odds ratio (aOR) = 3.12 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 1.15–8.45, P = .026). Lesion type (ref: microcystic): macrocystic: aOR = 8.95 (95% CI: 2.01–39.87, P = .004); mixed: aOR = 6.88 (95% CI: 1.32–35.81, P = .022). Pretreatment volume, age, depth, and location were not independent predictors in this model. Table 3 Multivariable logistic regression analysis of factors associated with a favorable outcome. Factor Adjusted odds ratio (aOR) 95% confidence interval P Treatment group (PPF vs PYM) 3.12 1.15–8.45 .026 Lesion type (ref: microcystic) Macrocystic 8.95 2.01–39.87 .004 Mixed 6.88 1.32–35.81 .022 Pretreatment volume (per log cm 3 ) 1.25 0.85–1.83 .258 Depth (deep vs superficial) 0.65 0.24–1.75 .395 Location (ref: head and neck) .450 Trunk 0.51 0.15–1.72 Limb 1.23 0.33–4.62 Axilla 0.18 0.02–1.92 Age (per mo) 1.00 0.99–1.01 .892 P < .05 was considered statistically significant. PPF = pingyangmycin–polidocanol foam, PYM = pingyangmycin. 3.4. Exploratory analysis of factors within the PPF group The analysis of factors associated with PPF sclerotherapy outcomes for hemorrhagic CLMs is detailed in Table 4 . In the PPF group, lesion type significantly influenced the response rate, with macrocystic and mixed types showing higher efficacy than microcystic types (Table 4 ). An important finding was the association between the number of treatment sessions and outcomes: a higher number of sessions (>3) was associated with a significantly increased incidence of adverse effects but a decreased rate of recurrence. These subgroup analyses are exploratory. Table 4 Exploratory analysis of factors influencing outcomes in the PPF group. PPF = pingyangmycin–polidocanol foam.
full_text_sections.4. DISCUSSION: 4. Discussion The management of CLMs in children has been revolutionized by sclerotherapy, which has largely replaced surgical resection due to its minimally invasive nature and favorable outcomes. [ 10 , 11 ] However, a significant subset of patients presents with the acute complication of intracapsular hemorrhage, leading to rapid enlargement, pain, skin discoloration, and potential compromise of vital functions. [ 5 , 8 , 9 ] This hemorrhagic transformation creates a distinct pathophysiological milieu that may theoretically impair the efficacy of standard sclerotherapy by diluting the sclerosant and forming a physical barrier between the agent and the target lymphatic endothelium. [ 12 , 18 ] Consequently, optimizing sclerotherapy protocols for this challenging presentation is a persistent clinical concern. While various single-agent and combination regimens have been explored for CLMs, [ 9 , 16 , 23 ] robust comparative data specifically focused on hemorrhagic lesions remain scarce. This retrospective cohort study was designed to address this gap by evaluating and comparing the clinical outcomes associated with a novel combination agent, PPF, versus the established PYM monotherapy in a well-defined pediatric cohort with imaging- and aspiration-confirmed intracapsular hemorrhage. Our principal findings indicate that, within the constraints of an observational study, treatment with PPF was associated with several favorable short-term clinical trends compared with PYM. Specifically, children receiving PPF exhibited a significantly greater median reduction in lesion volume after the first treatment session, required fewer median treatment sessions to achieve a clinical endpoint, reported higher rates of complete pain relief, and demonstrated greater family satisfaction scores. Importantly, these potential benefits were not accompanied by a statistically significant increase in overall adverse events or recurrence rates within the study’s follow-up period. Multivariable analysis further identified PPF treatment and macrocystic/mixed lesion morphology as independent factors associated with a good final treatment response (Achauer grade III/IV), consistent with established literature. [ 11 , 24 ] This is likely due to the technical challenge of adequately draining and distributing sclerosant within numerous small, septated microcysts. The superior initial volumetric response observed in the PPF group constitutes a pivotal finding. In the context of hemorrhagic CLMs, where patient distress and family anxiety are often high due to sudden onset and pain, achieving a pronounced early response can be critical for building therapeutic alliance and confidence in a potentially multi-session treatment plan. This enhanced initial efficacy can be plausibly attributed to the unique pharmacological and physical properties of the combined foam formulation. PYM acts as a potent fibrosing agent by inhibiting DNA synthesis and promoting the transformation of lymphatic endothelial cells into fibroblasts. [ 15 ] Polidocanol, a detergent sclerosant, exerts its effect by disrupting endothelial cell membranes, leading to thrombosis and fibrosis. [ 21 ] When combined and agitated into a foam using the Tessari method, [ 19 ] the resulting micro-bubbles create a stable structure with several theoretical advantages for hemorrhagic cysts. First, the foam physically displaces the hemorrhagic fluid, improving direct contact between the active agents and the cystic wall. Second, the foam significantly increases the surface area of the sclerosant in contact with the endothelium. Third, and most critically for hemorrhagic lesions, the foam’s stability prolongs the dwell time within the cavity, resisting rapid dilution by residual blood or serosanguineous fluid. [ 15 , 21 ] This mechanistic synergy likely explains why PPF was associated with a more substantial “first-hit” effect compared with PYM solution, which may be more susceptible to dilution in a bloody environment. Furthermore, the reduction in the median number of treatment sessions required in the PPF group (2 vs 3) represents a clinically meaningful outcome. Each sclerotherapy session entails general anesthesia, hospital resources, familial logistical burden, and inherent procedural risks. A regimen that achieves comparable or superior final results with fewer interventions directly enhances treatment efficiency, reduces cumulative healthcare costs, and minimizes psychosocial disruption for the child and family. This efficiency may stem from the more comprehensive initial endothelial ablation achieved by PPF, potentially reducing the need for repeated “clean-up” sessions to address residual or reperfused cystic compartments. The high rate of complete pain relief in the PPF group (73.6% vs 41.7% among those presenting with pain) is another significant patient-centered outcome. While the volumetric reduction itself contributes to pain relief by decreasing mass effect, the local anesthetic property intrinsic to polidocanol likely provides an additional, immediate analgesic benefit. [ 23 , 25 ] This dual action – simultaneously treating the lesion and alleviating a key symptom – enhances the therapeutic experience and may contribute to the higher satisfaction scores reported by families in the PPF group. [ 26 ] The overall adverse event rates were comparable (PPF: 18.9%, PYM: 27.0%), with no severe complications, indicating that the combination does not incur undue risk when properly administered. An important finding was the association within the PPF group between increased treatment sessions (>3) and higher adverse events (33.3% vs 5.3%). This underscores the principle of balancing efficacy with cumulative drug exposure. The PPF regimen, by potentially offering greater efficacy per session, may help clinicians navigate this risk-benefit balance more favorably, aiming to achieve the therapeutic goal within a minimal number of interventions. The exploratory analysis suggesting that more treatment sessions were associated with lower recurrence in the PPF group points toward a potential dose-response relationship for achieving durable fibrosis. However, the optimal “number needed to treat” to prevent recurrence while minimizing adverse events remains an individualized calculation, dependent on lesion characteristics and patient response. This study suggests PPF as an efficient first-line option for hemorrhagic CLMs, potentially enabling faster control with fewer procedures. For clinicians, the takeaway is that employing a foam-based combination agent from the outset may lead to faster lesion control, fewer overall procedures, and improved symptom management, particularly pain. This is especially relevant in resource-constrained environments or for families facing significant logistical hurdles. It reinforces that lesion morphology remains a key prognostic indicator; macrocystic/mixed lesions are excellent candidates for sclerotherapy even with hemorrhage, while microcystic lesions warrant cautious prognostication. The technical approach of image-guided aspiration followed by foam injection proved safe and effective, highlighting that optimal delivery is as important as agent selection. These findings must be interpreted considering the study’s limitations. The retrospective, nonrandomized design is susceptible to selection bias and confounding, despite statistical adjustments. Additionally, the nonrandomized treatment allocation may introduce temporal or learning-curve bias, as operators’ experience could have evolved over the study period. The modest sample size limits subgroup analysis power. The minimum 6-month follow-up is short for assessing long-term recurrence. The diagnosis of intracapsular hemorrhage relied on imaging findings and the gross appearance of aspirated fluid; cytological confirmation was not routinely obtained. Family satisfaction was assessed using a simple, non-validated scale, which, while clinically informative, limits comparability and should be interpreted with caution. Single-center experience may affect generalizability. It is important to clarify that PYM has limited availability and is not routinely approved for sclerotherapy outside Asia. [ 27 , 28 ] However, the broader clinical relevance of this study lies in the mechanistic and procedural principles of foam-based combination sclerotherapy – enhanced endothelial contact, improved intralesional distribution, and synergistic sclerosant action – which are universally applicable and can be adapted using locally available agents. [ 17 , 29 ] Thus, our findings contribute to the understanding of treatment protocols and response patterns beyond the specific drug used. A prospective, randomized controlled trial is paramount to establish comparative efficacy and safety. Long-term studies (>5 years) are needed to assess durability. Research should also focus on standardizing foam preparation, developing validated patient-reported outcome measures, and investigating imaging biomarkers for response prediction. In vitro studies on how hemorrhage components affect foam stability could further refine agent selection.
full_text_sections.5. CONCLUSION: 5. Conclusion In this retrospective analysis, PPF sclerotherapy was associated with greater initial volume reduction, fewer treatment sessions, and higher family satisfaction compared with PYM alone in pediatric CLMs with intracapsular hemorrhage. Macrocystic and mixed lesions showed better response to PPF. Prospective studies are warranted to validate these findings.
